figures.caption: Figure 6: Qualitative results for random inpainting on FFHQ. Even under large missing regions, ∆LPS generates plausible and visually faithful completions.
Figure 7: Qualitative results for Gaussian deblurring on FFHQ. ∆LPS reconstructs realistic facial structures while suppressing blur artifacts.

Figure 1: This paper introduces ∆LPS, a training-free approach for discrete posterior sampling using discrete diffusion priors. Shown here are results from various image restoration tasks across the MNIST, CIFAR, FFHQ datasets along with and spatial mapping. Project website: https://discretelps.github.io
Table 1: FFHQ inv. prob: PSNR (dB)↑ and LPIPS↓ with std. shown as subscript. Best shown in bold.
Table 2: Performance across inverse problems on MNIST. Token accuracy (%) and PSNR (dB). Both mean and std. dev. are reported. The best runs are shown in bold.
Figure 4: Qualitative results for the HDR reconstruction task on FFHQ. Each example contains the ground truth image, corrupted measurement, and reconstruction from ∆LPS.
Figure 5: Qualitative results for motion deblurring on FFHQ. Reconstructions remain sharp and semantically consistent despite severe motion corruption.
Table 3: CIFAR inverse problems. PSNR (dB) and SSIM with std as subscript

Figure 2: Comparison on FFHQ across multiple inverse problems, including HDR reconstruction, 4× super-resolution, Gaussian deblurring, and random inpainting. We compare ∆LPS against continuous diffusion-based baselines such as DPS and PSLD, as well as the discrete baseline APS. Across all tasks, reconstructions are visually comparable to those of the baselines, preserving facial details and perceptual quality even under severe corruptions.
Table 4: Performance across trajectory densities in the blind setting. Each regime reports F1 and IoU.

Figure 3: Qualitative results showing ∆LPS’s performance across various settings. On top, we show motion deblurring on FFHQ, outperforming the discrete APS baseline. In the middle, we evaluate on three different tasks on MNIST. The last row shows performance on the blind inverse setting, where the floorplan is estimated from walking trajectories.
Figure 8: Qualitative results for 4× superresolution on FFHQ. ∆LPS recovers fine facial details and realistic textures from low-resolution measurements.
Figure 9: Results for floorplan inference from human walking trajectories. ∆LPS reconstructs realistic floorplans from sparse measurements.
